title: Enforce Kubernetes Secrets protection for operator keypair — etcd encryption, RBAC lockdown, and immutable Secret
description: Harden the operator keypair Kubernetes Secret (cto-pay-operator-keypair) to prevent unauthorized access and modification. The operator keypair controls all settlement and treasury operations — compromise means complete fund theft. This task ensures etcd encryption at rest is verified, RBAC is scoped to only the controller ServiceAccount, and the Secret is marked immutable.
subtasks: Verify etcd encryption-at-rest is enabled on the cluster: inspect EncryptionConfiguration for secrets resource with aes-cbc or secretbox provider. Document the finding and escalate if not enabled.

Create a Role and RoleBinding in the cto namespace that restricts read access to the cto-pay-operator-keypair Secret to only the cto-pay controller ServiceAccount — no namespace-wide Secret read permissions.

Audit existing ClusterRoleBindings and RoleBindings in the cto namespace for any that grant broad secrets read access (e.g., get/list/watch on secrets resource); document and remediate any overly-permissive bindings.
Set `immutable: true` on the cto-pay-operator-keypair Secret manifest to prevent modification after creation.
Document the keypair protection measures in the security audit report, including a recommendation for KMS-backed secrets or HSM integration for production/mainnet deployment.
acceptance_criteria: etcd encryption-at-rest status is verified and documented (enabled with aes-cbc/secretbox, or gap documented with remediation plan)
A Role exists in the cto namespace granting get access to the cto-pay-operator-keypair Secret only, bound exclusively to the cto-pay controller ServiceAccount
No RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding grants namespace-wide secrets list/watch access to any non-system ServiceAccount in the cto namespace
The cto-pay-operator-keypair Secret manifest includes `immutable: true`
kubectl get secret cto-pay-operator-keypair -n cto -o jsonpath='{.immutable}' returns true

title: Document single operator keypair risk and implement compensating controls for hackathon scope
description: The entire program design uses a single OperatorConfig authority key that can settle tasks, pause/unpause, and perform refunds. If compromised, an attacker can drain all customer balances. For hackathon scope, this must be documented as a known limitation with a concrete production remediation roadmap. Compensating controls (monitoring alerts, rate-limit documentation) should be implemented where feasible within hackathon constraints.
subtasks: Create a security limitations document at docs/security-limitations.md covering the single-operator-key risk with attack scenarios: fraudulent settlement drain, malicious pause, unauthorized refunds.

Document the production remediation roadmap: (1) Squads Protocol multi-sig integration for operator authority, (2) timelock on high-value settlements >$1000, (3) on-chain rate limiting for operator settlements per time period, (4) separation of pause authority from settlement authority into different keys.
Add inline code comments in the OperatorConfig struct and settle_task/refund_task/pause instructions referencing the security limitation and the planned multi-sig upgrade path.

Define alerting thresholds for the monitoring system (Task 13): rapid settlement frequency (>N settlements per minute), large single settlements (>threshold amount), pause/unpause toggling within short timeframes.

Review the Anchor program to verify there is no instruction that allows the operator to arbitrarily modify customer balances without a corresponding TaskReceipt — confirm the settle_task → TaskReceipt PDA creation is atomic and non-bypassable.
acceptance_criteria: docs/security-limitations.md exists and covers single-operator-key risk with specific attack scenarios
Production roadmap includes multi-sig, timelock, rate-limiting, and authority separation with concrete implementation notes
Code comments exist on OperatorConfig, settle_task, refund_task, and pause/unpause referencing the security limitation
Alerting thresholds are defined for settlement frequency, large settlements, and pause toggling
Code review confirms no instruction exists that modifies customer balance without TaskReceipt creation

title: Secure operator keypair generation lifecycle — eliminate local file exfiltration window
description: The devnet operator keypair generation process (subtask 1005) creates an unencrypted keypair file on the developer's local disk during the generation-to-upload window. This task hardens the keypair generation lifecycle to minimize the exfiltration window and ensure proper cleanup.
subtasks: Modify scripts/setup-devnet.sh or create a new script scripts/generate-operator-keypair.sh that generates the keypair on a tmpfs/ramfs mount point (e.g., /dev/shm/) to avoid persisting to disk.
Replace `rm` with `shred -vfz -n 3 <keypair-file>` for all keypair file deletions to overwrite before unlinking.
Add a trap handler in the keypair generation script to ensure cleanup occurs even on script failure or interruption (trap 'shred -u /dev/shm/operator-keypair.json 2>/dev/null' EXIT).
Investigate and document whether 1Password CLI (`op`) can generate a Solana-compatible keypair directly without local file materialization; if feasible, implement this approach as the primary method.
Add a warning comment in the script and README that shell history may contain keypair-related pubkeys and should be cleared after key generation (`history -c` or selective removal).
acceptance_criteria: Keypair generation script uses /dev/shm or equivalent tmpfs for temporary keypair storage
All keypair file deletions use shred (not rm) with at least 3-pass overwrite
Trap handlers exist to clean up keypair files on script exit/failure/interruption
README or script comments document shell history cleanup procedure
If local file generation is required, the file exists for minimal duration with 600 permissions (umask 077)

title: Harden devnet setup script — input validation, variable quoting, file permissions, and cleanup traps
description: The scripts/setup-devnet.sh script (subtask 1006) needs hardening for input validation, shell security best practices, and sensitive file handling. While it uses set -euo pipefail, it lacks variable quoting, input sanitization, file permission restrictions, and trap-based cleanup.
subtasks: Add `umask 077` at the top of the script to ensure all created files (especially keypair files) have restrictive 600 permissions by default.
Quote all shell variable expansions: replace $VAR with "$VAR" throughout the entire script, including in solana CLI, spl-token CLI, and file path arguments.
Add input validation for the operator keypair path: verify the file exists, is a regular file, is readable, and contains valid JSON before use.
Add RPC URL format validation: verify the URL starts with https:// or http://, contains no shell metacharacters (;, |, &, $, backtick), and reject malicious URLs that could enable command injection.
Add trap handlers to clean up sensitive files (test customer keypairs) on script failure: `trap 'rm -f config/test-customer-*.json 2>/dev/null' ERR`.
Set explicit file permissions immediately after creating test customer keypair files: `chmod 600 config/test-customer-*.json`.
acceptance_criteria: umask 077 is set at the start of the script
shellcheck (or manual review) confirms all variable expansions are double-quoted
Operator keypair path is validated before use with existence, type, and readability checks
RPC URL is validated against a safe pattern before being passed to CLI tools
Trap handlers ensure cleanup of sensitive files on script failure
All generated keypair files have 600 permissions (verified by ls -la)

title: Implement Rust and TypeScript dependency pinning, lockfile commitment, and supply chain auditing in CI
description: Address supply chain security for both Rust/Cargo and TypeScript/npm dependency ecosystems. Pin exact versions, commit lockfiles, and add automated auditing to prevent dependency confusion and compromised package injection. The Solana ecosystem has had real supply chain incidents in both ecosystems.
subtasks: Pin exact versions in programs/cto-pay/Cargo.toml using the = prefix: `anchor-lang = "=0.30.1"`, `anchor-spl = "=0.30.1"`, `solana-program = "=1.18.x"` (determine exact patch version currently resolved and pin it).
Commit Cargo.lock to the repository (remove from .gitignore if present) — Anchor projects should always commit Cargo.lock for reproducible builds.
Pin exact versions in package.json and cli/package.json by removing ^ and ~ prefixes from all dependency version specifiers.
Commit bun.lockb (or package-lock.json if using npm) to the repository.
Add a CI step (in .github/workflows/) that runs `cargo audit` against the Cargo.lock to detect known CVEs in Rust dependencies.
Add a CI step that runs `bun audit` or `npm audit --audit-level=moderate` to detect known vulnerabilities in JS dependencies.
Pin the Rust toolchain version in rust-toolchain.toml (e.g., `channel = "1.75.0"`) for reproducible compilation.
Add a CI policy check (grep/conftest) that rejects Cargo.toml files using non-exact version specifiers (lines matching `"^` or `"~` in dependency declarations).
acceptance_criteria: All Cargo.toml dependency versions use exact pinning (= prefix)
Cargo.lock is committed to the repository and not in .gitignore
All package.json dependency versions are exact (no ^ or ~ prefixes)
bun.lockb or package-lock.json is committed to the repository
CI workflow includes cargo audit step that fails on known CVEs
CI workflow includes bun/npm audit step that fails on moderate+ vulnerabilities
rust-toolchain.toml exists with a pinned stable channel version
CI includes a policy check rejecting non-exact version pins in Cargo.toml

title: Implement SeaweedFS access control, authentication, and network policy restrictions
description: The SeaweedFS receipt bucket (cto-pay-receipts) is currently accessible without authentication over plain HTTP to any pod in the cluster. Receipt data contains settlement details including task IDs, amounts, and customer information. This task implements S3 authentication, creates dedicated credentials, and adds network-level restrictions.
subtasks: Enable SeaweedFS S3 authentication: configure access key and secret key for the S3 API gateway. Create a dedicated IAM-style credential pair for the cto-pay controller (access key: cto-pay-controller, with a generated secret key).

Store the SeaweedFS S3 credentials (access key and secret key) as a Kubernetes Secret via the ExternalSecret pipeline (1Password → OpenBao → ExternalSecret), creating secret cto-pay-seaweedfs-credentials in the cto namespace.
Update the cto-pay-infra-endpoints ConfigMap documentation and pod spec patterns to include the SeaweedFS credential secret reference.

Create a CiliumNetworkPolicy (or standard NetworkPolicy) in the cto namespace implementing default-deny ingress/egress, with explicit allowances for: (a) cto-pay pods → SeaweedFS on port 8333, (b) cto-pay pods → DNS (kube-dns), (c) cto-pay pods → external Solana RPC endpoints on port 443.

Evaluate SeaweedFS server-side encryption capabilities. If supported, enable it for the cto-pay-receipts bucket. If not supported, document a plan for application-layer encryption of receipt JSON using a key stored in OpenBao.
Restrict the SeaweedFS S3 bucket policy for cto-pay-receipts to only allow operations from the cto-pay-controller credential (deny all other access keys).
acceptance_criteria: SeaweedFS S3 API requires authentication — unauthenticated requests to the cto-pay-receipts bucket return 403
A dedicated SeaweedFS credential exists for the cto-pay controller, stored as a Kubernetes Secret via ExternalSecret
A CiliumNetworkPolicy or NetworkPolicy exists in the cto namespace with default-deny and explicit allow rules for cto-pay pods
Pods outside the cto-pay deployment cannot reach SeaweedFS port 8333 (verified by attempting access from a test pod)
Receipt data encryption status is documented — either server-side encryption is enabled or an application-layer encryption plan exists with OpenBao key reference
The SeaweedFS bucket policy restricts access to only the cto-pay-controller credential

title: Add on-chain vault solvency check in withdraw instruction and monitoring alert
description: The refund_task instruction credits the customer's ledger balance without transferring tokens back to the vault, creating potential insolvency where the vault cannot cover all withdrawal requests. This task adds an on-chain safety check in the withdraw instruction and defines monitoring for vault solvency.
subtasks: Add a constraint or runtime check in the withdraw instruction that verifies `vault.amount >= amount` before executing the SPL transfer — if the vault has insufficient tokens, the withdrawal should fail with a descriptive error (add a new CtoPayError variant: VaultInsolvent).
Add the VaultInsolvent error variant to errors.rs with message 'Vault has insufficient tokens to cover withdrawal'.

Document the ledger-only refund accounting model in docs/security-limitations.md: explain that refund_task credits balance without transferring tokens, the operator must replenish the vault independently, and the on-chain check prevents customers from withdrawing more than the vault holds.

Define a monitoring alert specification: vault_balance < sum_of_all_customer_balances should trigger a critical alert. Document how to compute this off-chain (query vault token account balance and iterate/aggregate CustomerBalance accounts).
Add a unit test verifying that the VaultInsolvent error is returned when attempting to withdraw more than the vault's token balance (even if the customer's ledger balance is sufficient).
acceptance_criteria: The withdraw instruction includes a check that vault.amount >= withdrawal amount before CPI transfer
A VaultInsolvent error variant exists in CtoPayError
A test demonstrates that withdrawal fails with VaultInsolvent when vault tokens are insufficient despite adequate customer ledger balance
docs/security-limitations.md documents the refund accounting model and vault solvency assumptions
Monitoring alert specification exists for vault solvency with computation methodology

title: Add Anchor event emission for all security-critical on-chain operations
description: The Anchor program currently emits no events, making it impossible to efficiently monitor for suspicious on-chain activity (rapid settlement draining, pause toggling, bulk refunds). This task adds #[event] structs and emit! calls for all fund-moving and administrative operations to enable off-chain indexing and real-time security monitoring.
subtasks: Define event structs in a new file programs/cto-pay/src/events.rs: SettlementEvent { task_id_hash: [u8; 32], customer: Pubkey, amount: u64, fee_amount: u64, timestamp: i64 }, RefundEvent { task_id_hash: [u8; 32], customer: Pubkey, amount: u64 }, DepositEvent { customer: Pubkey, amount: u64 }, WithdrawEvent { customer: Pubkey, amount: u64 }, PauseEvent { authority: Pubkey }, UnpauseEvent { authority: Pubkey }.
Add `emit!(SettlementEvent { ... })` at the end of the settle_task handler after all state updates and CPI transfers.
Add `emit!(RefundEvent { ... })` at the end of the refund_task handler.
Add `emit!(DepositEvent { ... })` at the end of the deposit handler and `emit!(WithdrawEvent { ... })` at the end of the withdraw handler.
Add `emit!(PauseEvent { ... })` and `emit!(UnpauseEvent { ... })` in the respective pause/unpause handlers.
Update the test suite to verify events are emitted: check transaction logs for event discriminators or use Anchor's event parser in tests.
Verify the IDL includes all event definitions after `anchor build`.
acceptance_criteria: events.rs module exists with all 6 event structs using #[event] attribute
Every fund-moving instruction (deposit, withdraw, settle_task, refund_task) emits its corresponding event
Both pause and unpause instructions emit their respective events
anchor build generates an IDL that includes all 6 events in the events section
At least one integration test verifies that a SettlementEvent is emitted with correct fields after settle_task

title: Implement baseline default-deny CiliumNetworkPolicy for cto namespace during development
description: Network policies are currently deferred to scale tasks, leaving the cto namespace completely open during development and testing. Any compromised pod in the cluster can access SeaweedFS, operator keypair secrets, and other cto-pay infrastructure. This task implements a minimal default-deny network policy immediately as part of infrastructure bootstrap, rather than deferring to production hardening.
subtasks: Create a default-deny CiliumNetworkPolicy for the cto namespace that blocks all ingress and egress traffic by default.
Add an egress allow rule for DNS resolution (kube-dns/coredns on port 53 UDP/TCP in kube-system namespace).
Add an egress allow rule for cto-pay pods to reach SeaweedFS on port 8333 within the cluster.
Add an egress allow rule for cto-pay pods to reach external Solana RPC endpoints on port 443 (HTTPS).
Add an ingress allow rule for any necessary service exposure (e.g., if the controller exposes health/metrics endpoints).
Test the network policy by deploying a test pod in the cto namespace and verifying it cannot reach SeaweedFS (while the cto-pay labeled pods can).
Document the network policy in the infrastructure README with instructions for adding new allow rules as services are added.
acceptance_criteria: A CiliumNetworkPolicy with default-deny for both ingress and egress exists in the cto namespace
cto-pay pods can resolve DNS, reach SeaweedFS, and reach external RPC endpoints
Pods without cto-pay labels in the cto namespace cannot reach SeaweedFS (verified by test)
The policy is applied as part of Task 1 infrastructure bootstrap, not deferred to scale tasks
Infrastructure README documents the network policy and how to extend it

title: Reduce ExternalSecret refresh interval for security-critical secrets and document incident response
description: The ExternalSecret for the operator keypair uses a 1-hour refresh interval, meaning a compromised and rotated key will remain active in Kubernetes for up to 1 hour. This task reduces the refresh interval for security-critical secrets and documents the incident response procedure for keypair compromise.
subtasks: Update the cto-pay-operator-keypair ExternalSecret refreshInterval from 1h to 5m (5 minutes) to reduce the window between secret rotation in 1Password/OpenBao and Kubernetes Secret update.
Update the cto-pay-helius-api-key ExternalSecret refreshInterval from 1h to 15m.
Document the manual emergency refresh procedure: `kubectl annotate externalsecret cto-pay-operator-keypair -n cto force-sync=$(date +%s) --overwrite` to trigger immediate sync.

Create an incident response runbook at docs/incident-response-keypair-compromise.md covering: (1) rotate key in 1Password, (2) trigger forced ExternalSecret refresh, (3) restart cto-pay controller pods to pick up new secret, (4) pause the on-chain program, (5) verify no unauthorized settlements occurred during compromise window.
Add a monitoring check that alerts if ExternalSecret sync status shows errors or if the last sync time exceeds 2x the refresh interval.
acceptance_criteria: cto-pay-operator-keypair ExternalSecret has refreshInterval of 5m or less
cto-pay-helius-api-key ExternalSecret has refreshInterval of 15m or less
docs/incident-response-keypair-compromise.md exists with step-by-step runbook
Manual forced sync command is documented and tested
Monitoring specification includes ExternalSecret sync health check

title: Resolve security audit tooling decision and ensure structured static analysis capability
description: Task 7 (security audit) has an unresolved decision point about Soteria availability, with no finalized fallback plan. If no automated static analysis tool is available, the audit relies entirely on manual review and basic grep patterns, which may miss subtle vulnerabilities. This task resolves the tooling decision before the audit begins, ensuring at least one structured static analysis tool is operational.
subtasks: Evaluate sec3 X-ray availability and compatibility with the cto-pay Anchor program: install, configure, and run against the program source. Document results and any compatibility issues.
Evaluate Semgrep with community Solana/Anchor rules: install Semgrep, find and configure relevant Solana security rules (e.g., from Trail of Bits or community rulesets), run against program source.

If neither sec3 X-ray nor Semgrep with Solana rules is viable, create custom Rust AST analysis scripts using cargo-expand or syn crate to detect: (a) unchecked arithmetic, (b) missing signer checks, (c) init_if_needed usage, (d) missing owner validation on AccountInfo.
Document the chosen tooling stack in audit/tooling-decision.md with rationale, installation instructions, and sample output.
Run the chosen tool(s) against the cto-pay program and produce initial findings to validate the tool is functional before the formal audit (Task 7) begins.
acceptance_criteria: At least one structured static analysis tool (beyond grep) is operational and validated against the cto-pay program
audit/tooling-decision.md exists documenting the chosen tool, alternatives evaluated, and rationale
The chosen tool produces parseable output when run against the cto-pay Anchor program source
Initial findings from the tool run are documented (even if zero findings, confirming the tool executed successfully)
The security audit (Task 7) can proceed with confidence that automated analysis supplements manual review

title: Implement CI policy check preventing API keys and secrets in ConfigMap manifests
description: Close the decision point definitively: the Helius API key must never be stored in a ConfigMap. Add a CI check that rejects any ConfigMap YAML containing API key patterns, preventing regression if the decision point in Task 1 is revisited. This also serves as a general defense against accidental secret inclusion in non-Secret Kubernetes resources.
subtasks: Create a CI script or conftest policy at ci/policies/no-secrets-in-configmap.sh (or .rego for conftest) that scans all ConfigMap YAML files for patterns: 'api-key', 'api_key', 'apikey', 'API_KEY', 'secret', 'password', 'token' (case-insensitive, excluding known safe keys like 'token_program').
Add the policy check as a CI step in .github/workflows/ that runs on every PR affecting infra/gitops/ or config/ directories.
Add a comment in the cto-pay-infra-endpoints ConfigMap YAML documenting that SOLANA_RPC_URL must NOT contain the API key — the controller concatenates the key from the Secret at runtime.
Update the README or contributing guide to document that API keys and secrets must use ExternalSecret/Secret resources, never ConfigMaps.
Test the CI check by creating a test ConfigMap with an embedded api-key value and verifying the check fails.
acceptance_criteria: A CI policy check exists that scans ConfigMap YAMLs for secret-like patterns
The check runs automatically on PRs affecting infrastructure YAML files
A test case demonstrates the check correctly rejects a ConfigMap containing 'api-key'
The cto-pay-infra-endpoints ConfigMap YAML contains a comment prohibiting API key inclusion
Documentation states the API key separation requirement

title: Document internal TLS gap and implement production TLS remediation plan
description: All internal traffic including receipt uploads and configuration synchronization uses unencrypted HTTP within the cluster. While acceptable for hackathon scope, this must be documented as a known limitation with a concrete remediation plan for any environment handling real funds.
subtasks: Document the internal TLS gap in docs/security-limitations.md: list all internal HTTP endpoints (SeaweedFS S3 API, any inter-pod communication) and the data flowing over them.

Create a TLS remediation plan document at docs/tls-remediation-plan.md covering: (1) deploy internal CA certificates as part of infrastructure bootstrap, (2) configure SeaweedFS with TLS, (3) evaluate Cilium encryption or Linkerd for transparent mTLS between pods.
Add comments in the cto-pay-infra-endpoints ConfigMap noting that HTTP endpoints must be upgraded to HTTPS before handling real funds.
Evaluate whether Cilium's transparent encryption (WireGuard or IPsec) is already enabled on the cluster, document the finding.
acceptance_criteria: docs/security-limitations.md documents all internal HTTP endpoints and data classification
docs/tls-remediation-plan.md exists with concrete steps for internal TLS deployment
ConfigMap YAML contains comments noting HTTP→HTTPS upgrade requirement
Cluster's Cilium encryption status is documented
